## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Colorado | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/colorado.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Colorado

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Colorado? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for CO filers.

Home › States › Colorado

Homestead Exemption in Colorado

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Colorado, the homestead exemption is $250,000 ($350,000 if elderly or disabled).

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Colorado for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Colorado homestead: $250,000 ($350,000 if elderly or disabled). If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Colorado

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Colorado:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Colorado. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $250,000 ($350,000 if elderly or disabled)

Median income (single filer): $57,688. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Colorado's $15,000 vehicle exemption is among the highest in the country. The elderly/disabled receive enhanced amounts.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Connecticut | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/connecticut.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Connecticut

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Connecticut? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for CT filers.

Home › States › Connecticut

Homestead Exemption in Connecticut

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Connecticut, the homestead exemption is $75,000.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Federal alternative: Since Connecticut allows federal exemptions, you can alternatively claim the federal homestead exemption of $27,900 per person ($55,800 for a married couple filing jointly). Compare both sets to determine which is more favorable.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Connecticut for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Connecticut homestead: $75,000. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Connecticut

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Connecticut:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Connecticut. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $75,000

Median income (single filer): $60,008. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Federal exemptions may be more favorable than Connecticut state exemptions for renters due to the larger federal wildcard.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Delaware | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/delaware.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Delaware

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Delaware? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for DE filers.

Home › States › Delaware

Homestead Exemption in Delaware

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Delaware, the homestead exemption is $125,000.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Delaware for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Delaware homestead: $125,000. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Delaware

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Delaware:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Delaware. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $125,000

Median income (single filer): $51,348. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Delaware's $15,000 vehicle exemption is generous compared to most East Coast states.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in District of Columbia | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/district-of-columbia.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in District of Columbia

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in District of Columbia? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for DC filers.

Home › States › District of Columbia

Homestead Exemption in District of Columbia

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In District of Columbia, the homestead exemption is Unlimited (with residency requirement).

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Federal alternative: Since District of Columbia allows federal exemptions, you can alternatively claim the federal homestead exemption of $27,900 per person ($55,800 for a married couple filing jointly). Compare both sets to determine which is more favorable.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in District of Columbia for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

District of Columbia homestead: Unlimited (with residency requirement). If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in District of Columbia

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in District of Columbia:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Columbia. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: Unlimited (with residency requirement)

Median income (single filer): $65,890. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

DC's unlimited homestead exemption makes it one of the most debtor-friendly jurisdictions in the country.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Florida | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/florida.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Florida

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Florida? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for FL filers.

Home › States › Florida

Homestead Exemption in Florida

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Florida, the homestead exemption is Unlimited (1/2 acre urban, 160 acres rural).

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Florida for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Florida homestead: Unlimited (1/2 acre urban, 160 acres rural). If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Florida

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Florida:

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Middle District, Southern District. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: Unlimited (1/2 acre urban, 160 acres rural)

Median income (single filer): $48,817. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Florida's unlimited homestead exemption (subject to acreage limits) is one of the most powerful in the nation, but vehicle protection is very low at $1,000.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Georgia | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/georgia.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home

Chapter 13
Homestead

By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Georgia

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Georgia? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for GA filers.

Home › States › Georgia

Homestead Exemption in Georgia

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Georgia, the homestead exemption is $21,500.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Georgia for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Georgia homestead: $21,500. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Georgia

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Georgia:

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Middle District, Southern District. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $21,500

Median income (single filer): $46,492. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Georgia allows unused homestead exemption to be applied as a wildcard, providing flexibility for renters.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Hawaii | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/hawaii.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Hawaii

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Hawaii? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for HI filers.

Home › States › Hawaii

Homestead Exemption in Hawaii

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Hawaii, the homestead exemption is $30,000 (head of household: $48,000).

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Federal alternative: Since Hawaii allows federal exemptions, you can alternatively claim the federal homestead exemption of $27,900 per person ($55,800 for a married couple filing jointly). Compare both sets to determine which is more favorable.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Hawaii for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Hawaii homestead: $30,000 (head of household: $48,000). If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Hawaii

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Hawaii:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Hawaii. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $30,000 (head of household: $48,000)

Median income (single filer): $58,370. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Federal exemptions are often more favorable than Hawaii state exemptions due to Hawaii's low homestead.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

FAQ ·
By State

This site provides general information only and does not constitute legal advice. No attorney-client relationship is created by using this site. Consult a licensed attorney for advice on your specific situation.
A project of the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit (determination pending).

---

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Idaho | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/idaho.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Idaho

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Idaho? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for ID filers.

Home › States › Idaho

Homestead Exemption in Idaho

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Idaho, the homestead exemption is $175,000.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Idaho for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Idaho homestead: $175,000. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Idaho

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Idaho:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Idaho. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $175,000

Median income (single filer): $46,044. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Idaho's $175,000 homestead and $10,000 vehicle exemptions provide strong protection for typical families.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Research Platform ·

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Illinois | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/illinois.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Illinois

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Illinois? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for IL filers.

Home › States › Illinois

Homestead Exemption in Illinois

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Illinois, the homestead exemption is $15,000.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Illinois for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Illinois homestead: $15,000. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Illinois

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Illinois:

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Central District, Southern District. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $15,000

Median income (single filer): $52,359. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Illinois has notably low homestead and vehicle exemptions compared to neighboring states. The wildcard can help bridge the gap.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Indiana | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/indiana.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Indiana

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Indiana? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for IN filers.

Home › States › Indiana

Homestead Exemption in Indiana

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Indiana, the homestead exemption is $22,750.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Indiana for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Indiana homestead: $22,750. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Indiana

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Indiana:

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Southern District. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $22,750

Median income (single filer): $44,966. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Indiana uses an aggregate personal property exemption rather than item-by-item limits, offering some flexibility.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Iowa | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/iowa.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Iowa

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Iowa? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for IA filers.

Home › States › Iowa

Homestead Exemption in Iowa

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Iowa, the homestead exemption is Unlimited (1/2 acre urban, 40 acres rural).

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Iowa for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Iowa homestead: Unlimited (1/2 acre urban, 40 acres rural). If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Iowa

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Iowa:

Bankruptcy districts: Northern District, Southern District. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: Unlimited (1/2 acre urban, 40 acres rural)

Median income (single filer): $46,537. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Iowa's unlimited homestead exemption (with acreage limits) provides powerful home protection. Vehicle exemption is moderate.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Kansas | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/kansas.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Kansas

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Kansas? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for KS filers.

Home › States › Kansas

Homestead Exemption in Kansas

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Kansas, the homestead exemption is Unlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural).

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Kansas for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Kansas homestead: Unlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural). If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Kansas

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Kansas:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Kansas. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: Unlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural)

Median income (single filer): $46,929. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Kansas combines an unlimited homestead with a $20,000 vehicle exemption -- among the most generous vehicle protections in the U.S.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Kentucky | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/kentucky.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Kentucky

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Kentucky? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for KY filers.

Home › States › Kentucky

Homestead Exemption in Kentucky

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Kentucky, the homestead exemption is $5,000.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Federal alternative: Since Kentucky allows federal exemptions, you can alternatively claim the federal homestead exemption of $27,900 per person ($55,800 for a married couple filing jointly). Compare both sets to determine which is more favorable.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Kentucky for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Kentucky homestead: $5,000. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Kentucky

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Kentucky:

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Western District. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $5,000

Median income (single filer): $42,894. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Kentucky's state exemptions are among the lowest in the nation. Most filers benefit from choosing federal exemptions instead.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Louisiana | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/louisiana.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Louisiana

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Louisiana? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for LA filers.

Home › States › Louisiana

Homestead Exemption in Louisiana

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Louisiana, the homestead exemption is $35,000.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Louisiana for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Louisiana homestead: $35,000. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Louisiana

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Louisiana:

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Middle District, Western District. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $35,000

Median income (single filer): $41,587. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Louisiana is unusual -- it has no specific vehicle exemption. Vehicles may be partially protected under general property rules.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Maine | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/maine.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Maine

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Maine? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for ME filers.

Home › States › Maine

Homestead Exemption in Maine

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Maine, the homestead exemption is $80,000 ($160,000 if age 60+ or disabled).

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Maine for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Maine homestead: $80,000 ($160,000 if age 60+ or disabled). If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Maine

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Maine:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Maine. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $80,000 ($160,000 if age 60+ or disabled)

Median income (single filer): $45,814. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Maine provides enhanced exemptions for elderly and disabled filers, doubling the homestead protection.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Maryland | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/maryland.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Maryland

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Maryland? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for MD filers.

Home › States › Maryland

Homestead Exemption in Maryland

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Maryland, the homestead exemption is $25,150.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Maryland for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Maryland homestead: $25,150. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Maryland

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Maryland:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Maryland. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $25,150

Median income (single filer): $56,225. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Maryland's $6,000 wildcard exemption provides useful flexibility for protecting miscellaneous assets.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Massachusetts | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/massachusetts.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Massachusetts

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Massachusetts? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for MA filers.

Home › States › Massachusetts

Homestead Exemption in Massachusetts

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Massachusetts, the homestead exemption is $500,000 (must file declaration).

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Federal alternative: Since Massachusetts allows federal exemptions, you can alternatively claim the federal homestead exemption of $27,900 per person ($55,800 for a married couple filing jointly). Compare both sets to determine which is more favorable.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Massachusetts for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Massachusetts homestead: $500,000 (must file declaration). If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Massachusetts

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Massachusetts:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Massachusetts. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $500,000 (must file declaration)

Median income (single filer): $60,413. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Massachusetts has a strong $500,000 homestead, but you must file a declaration at the registry of deeds before filing bankruptcy for the full amount.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Michigan | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/michigan.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Michigan

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Michigan? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for MI filers.

Home › States › Michigan

Homestead Exemption in Michigan

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Michigan, the homestead exemption is $44,625.

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Federal alternative: Since Michigan allows federal exemptions, you can alternatively claim the federal homestead exemption of $27,900 per person ($55,800 for a married couple filing jointly). Compare both sets to determine which is more favorable.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Michigan for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Michigan homestead: $44,625. If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Michigan

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Michigan:

Bankruptcy districts: Eastern District, Western District. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $44,625

Median income (single filer): $47,522. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Michigan's option to apply unused homestead as wildcard makes it flexible, especially for renters who can redirect large amounts to other property.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Minnesota | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/minnesota.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com
☰

Chapter 13
Homestead

By State

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Minnesota

Can you keep your house in bankruptcy in Minnesota? Homestead exemptions, mortgage rules, lien stripping, and foreclosure protection for MN filers.

Home › States › Minnesota

Homestead Exemption in Minnesota

The homestead exemption is the primary tool for protecting your home in bankruptcy. In Minnesota, the homestead exemption is $450,000 ($1,125,000 agricultural).

This exemption protects equity in your primary residence. Equity means the fair market value minus the total of all mortgages, liens, and encumbrances. If your equity is within the exemption limit, the Chapter 7 trustee cannot sell your home.

Federal alternative: Since Minnesota allows federal exemptions, you can alternatively claim the federal homestead exemption of $27,900 per person ($55,800 for a married couple filing jointly). Compare both sets to determine which is more favorable.

Two-year residency rule: Under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(b)(3), if you have not lived in Minnesota for at least 730 days before filing, you may be required to use the exemptions of the state where you lived during the 180 days before that 730-day period. This prevents forum shopping.

Chapter 7: Will the Trustee Sell Your Home?

In Chapter 7, the trustee examines whether your home has non-exempt equity. If it does, the trustee can sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors.

However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases -- meaning the trustee finds nothing worth liquidating. The trustee will not sell your home unless the non-exempt equity is substantial enough to generate meaningful funds after paying the costs of sale (broker commissions, closing costs, trustee fees).

Practical threshold: Even if you have equity slightly above the exemption, the trustee may abandon the property if the costs of sale would consume the surplus. There is no guaranteed threshold -- it depends on the trustee's judgment and local market conditions.

Minnesota homestead: $450,000 ($1,125,000 agricultural). If you are close to this limit, consider whether Chapter 13 (which always lets you keep your home) is a safer choice.

Chapter 13: Stop Foreclosure and Catch Up

Chapter 13 is designed to let you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Key benefits:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread across your repayment plan. You resume regular payments going forward while the arrears are paid through the plan.

Automatic stay stops foreclosure: Filing Chapter 13 immediately halts foreclosure proceedings under 11 U.S.C. Section 362.

Lien stripping: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, Chapter 13 allows you to strip (remove) junior liens (second mortgages, HELOCs) entirely. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt.

Modification: While bankruptcy does not modify first mortgage terms, the payment plan gives you breathing room to negotiate with your lender.

To keep your home in Chapter 13, you must continue making regular mortgage payments on time throughout the plan, plus plan payments that include the arrearage cure.

Mortgage and Foreclosure Rules in Minnesota

Each state has different foreclosure procedures, timelines, and protections. Key considerations in Minnesota:

Bankruptcy districts: District of Minnesota. Local rules may affect how mortgage creditors interact with the bankruptcy court.

Homestead exemption: $450,000 ($1,125,000 agricultural)

Median income (single filer): $53,266. This affects whether you qualify for Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13.

Minnesota's $450,000 homestead is among the highest fixed-amount exemptions in the country. Agricultural property gets even more.

If you are facing foreclosure, timing matters. Filing bankruptcy too late (after a foreclosure sale) means the automatic stay cannot help you recover the property. File before the sale date.

Best outcome: Most homeowners keep their home through bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the exemption protects equity. In Chapter 13, you cure arrears and stop foreclosure. The key is filing before it is too late.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Mississippi | keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com -- Keep My House
Source: https://keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com/states/mississippi.html

keepmyhouseinbankruptcy.com

Chapter 13
Homestead
